EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Chinese Peak fire began 1 mile south of Pocatello on July 19th and was 100 percent contained by July 21st.   The fire was human caused by sparks from a grinder landing in dry grass near Sparkman Road.   The fire burned 1,546 acres of grass, brush and juniper.   It began at 3 pm MDT on July 19th.   No structures were threatened and no evacuations occurred.   Some road closures were in effect on the 19th along Barton Road to Chinese Peak.

No structures were threatened and no evacuations occurred.   Some road closures were in effect on the 19th along Barton Road to Chinese Peak.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific storm system brought heavy snow and strong winds to much of Eastern Idaho from very late on December 31st through New Year's Day. The heaviest snow fell in the Centennial Mountains/Island Park area where 10 to 20 inches were common. The Upper Snake Plain received widespread 6 to 8 inch snow amounts with parking restrictions instituted in Idaho Falls on the 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snowfall fell on New Year's Day.   Widespread amounts of 5 to 8 inches were recorded in the Upper Snake River Plain including 6 to 8 inches in Idaho Falls, 5 to 7 inches in Rexburg, 6 inches in Rigby, 7 inches in Menan.   The city of Idaho Falls enacted parking restrictions on New Year's Day to clear room for snow plows.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front brought the combination of light snow and wind and various road closures to southeast Idaho.  Snowfall amounts were not heavy and in the 1 to 4 inch range.  Roads closed were Idaho route 33 from Newdale to Tetonia, Idaho 47 from Ashton to Warm River, Idaho 32 from Ashton to Tetonia, US 26 from Ririe to Swan Valley, Idaho 81 from Malta to the Cotterell Port of Entry, Idaho 77 from Malta to Albion, and interstate 84 from Burley to the Utah border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front brought only 1 to 3 inches of snow but combined with wind gusts up to 60 mph made travel extremely hazardous.   Idaho Route 77 from Malta to Albion closed due to the drifting snow and zero visibility.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front brought the combination of light snow and wind and various road closures to southeast Idaho.  Snowfall amounts were not heavy and in the 1 to 4 inch range.  Roads closed were Idaho route 33 from Newdale to Tetonia, Idaho 47 from Ashton to Warm River, Idaho 32 from Ashton to Tetonia, US 26 from Ririe to Swan Valley, Idaho 81 from Malta to the Cotterell Port of Entry, Idaho 77 from Malta to Albion, and interstate 84 from Burley to the Utah border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through the Upper Snake River Plain with high winds and light snow making travel extremely hazardous.   Snow amounts generally only ranged from a half inch to 3 inches but combined with 40 to 50 mph wind gusts made snow removal difficult.   Idaho Route 33 closed from Newdale to Tetonia and US Highway 26 was closed from Ririe to Swan Valley.   The following schools were closed on the 17th:  Bonneville Joint District 93, Idaho Falls District 91, and Jefferson District 251.

Interstate 84 was closed for much of the day from 11 miles east of Heyburn to the Utah border due to the wind gusts causing zero visibility and significant drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic front moving in from Canada collided with moisture from a trough off the Pacific Coast from the afternoon of March 13th into the morning of March 14th. The cold air seeping through the Idaho northern valleys brought areas of terrain channeled strong winds, which downed numerous trees, many of which damaged homes, and produced thousands of power outages. Near Sagle, a tree fell on a trailer home and injured a child. ||The interaction of the cold air with the moisture also created a frontal boundary along which generally 2 to 4 inches fell with locally higher amounts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported a 30 foot spruce tree was blown down by local channeled winds in Dover. Scattered power outages were also reported in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 300 yard avalanche which was 6 to 7 feet caused the death of a man at Pebble Creek Ski Resort on March 15th. The avalanche was believed to be caused by heavy, wet snow at high elevations of the ski resort.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the day of February 4th light snow developed along a stationary front laying west to east across central Washington and into the Idaho Panhandle. This first wave of moisture brought with generally light snow accumulations, but this snow melted and subsequently froze on roadways causing multiple collisions along interstate 90 and caused authorities to close the highway near Post Falls for 2 hours . ||A second wave of deeper moisture enhanced along this boundary during the evening and overnight hours and brought heavy accumulations to the Idaho Palouse and Central Panhandle Mountains of north Idaho by the morning of February 5th when accumulating snow snow began to  taper off or turn to rain. Valley snow accumulations of 4 to 6 inches were common near and along this stationary front, with lighter but still significant accumulations of 2 to 4 inches noted across the northern Idaho Panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow accumulations during the afternoon of February 4th melted on the surface of Interstate 90 and then subsequently re-froze into a layer of ice. Law enforcement reported numerous slide offs and collisions prompting the closure of the westbound lane of interstate 90 for 2 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Beginning February 6th through the 8th, a stalled atmosphere river was followed by shortwave trough bringing record breaking moderate to heavy precipitation. This lead to a mix of heavy rain and wet snow which caused sharp rises in small streams and creeks across parts of central Idaho Panhandle into the Camas Prairie. With at or above freezing temperatures and high snow levels, snow melt was another contributor to the rises during this period. ||Numerous sites reached 24 hour total rainfall of 1 or greater on February 6th. The ASOS station at Lewiston Airport had a record breaking rain fall of 1.24. Numerous MESONET sites in Nez Perce and Lewis counties received over 1 (ex. 5 NW Winchester, 1.56; 12 S Waha, 1.56; 4.1 SSW Lapwai, 1.47). ||Late February 7th, there were reports of flooding in small streams and rivers in northern Nez Perce county. Trained spotters reported flooding on Lapwai Creek, Potlatch River, Mission Creek, and Cottonwood Creek. In Latah County, sharp rises on Paradise Creek reached flood stage in Moscow leading to water overflowing out of storm drains. Minor flooding was reported with the crest occurring late Friday evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The USGS Stream Gage on Paradise Creek at Moscow reported a rapid rise during the afternoon of February 7th. The creek rose above the Flood Stage of 9.2 feet at 9:30 PM PST on February 7th, crested at a stage of 9.3 feet at 1:15 AM February 8th, and dropped back below Flood Stage at 2:00 AM February 8th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Beginning February 6th through the 8th, a stalled atmosphere river was followed by shortwave trough bringing record breaking moderate to heavy precipitation. This lead to a mix of heavy rain and wet snow which caused sharp rises in small streams and creeks across parts of central Idaho Panhandle into the Camas Prairie. With at or above freezing temperatures and high snow levels, snow melt was another contributor to the rises during this period. ||Numerous sites reached 24 hour total rainfall of 1 or greater on February 6th. The ASOS station at Lewiston Airport had a record breaking rain fall of 1.24. Numerous MESONET sites in Nez Perce and Lewis counties received over 1 (ex. 5 NW Winchester, 1.56; 12 S Waha, 1.56; 4.1 SSW Lapwai, 1.47). ||Late February 7th, there were reports of flooding in small streams and rivers in northern Nez Perce county. Trained spotters reported flooding on Lapwai Creek, Potlatch River, Mission Creek, and Cottonwood Creek. In Latah County, sharp rises on Paradise Creek reached flood stage in Moscow leading to water overflowing out of storm drains. Minor flooding was reported with the crest occurring late Friday evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding of Paradise Creek was reported in Moscow. On Bridge Street and Roosevelt Street there was minor flooding resulting from water overflowing out of storm drains. No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system in southern British Columbia carried a cold front through the Pacific Northwest on January 31st and February 1st. The windy conditions developed as the region became wedged between the leading warm front and incoming cold front. There were reports of winds reaching 40 - 55 mph with gusts up to 65 mph. While isolated damage reports were received from northeast Washington, the main focus of widespread downed trees and power outages occurred in north Idaho.  Early February 1st, strong winds toppled trees in Bonner and Kootenai Counties leading to damaged homes, blocked roadways, and power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The west end of Wrenco Loop Road near Highway 2 was closed due to trees and power lines blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific cold front moved across the Northern Rockies during the late afternoon and early evening hours causing wintry impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The same squall line with the cold front continued to progress southward in Lemhi County. Whiteout conditions were reported along Highway 28.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific cold front moved across the Northern Rockies during the late afternoon and early evening hours causing wintry impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As the cold front advanced south, the snow band stayed organized via radar, therefore a snow squall warning was issued for the city of Salmon, ID. A known observer and snow plow operator called in whiteout conditions and reported snowfall accumulations of 2.5 inches. The AWOS at the Lemhi County Airport measured winds changing from SE at 5 kts to NNW 22 kts, gusting to 30 kts in 30 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific cold front moved across the Northern Rockies during the late afternoon and early evening hours causing wintry impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Radar measured up to 30 dBZ reflectivity at 20 thousand feet MSL in a band parallel to a cold front over Highway 93 from Lost Trail Pass south to North Fork, ID. After a tweet reported whiteout conditions at the pass, a snow squall warning was issued, which was an upgrade to the previous special weather statement. In addition, Idaho Transportation Department cameras confirmed whiteout conditions at the pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Interstate 15 closed due to blowing dust at 2:30 PM MDT on May 3rd from milepost 119 to milepost 135 between Idaho Falls and Roberts.   Sustained winds of 25 to 35 mph with gusts of 35 to 45 mph caused visibility to near zero in that stretch as dust from freshly plowed fields affected that stretch of interstate.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Interstate 15 closed due to blowing dust at 2:30 PM MDT on May 3rd from milepost 119 to milepost 135 between Idaho Falls and Roberts.   Sustained winds of 25 to 35 mph with gusts of 35 to 45 mph caused visibility to near zero in that stretch as dust from freshly plowed fields affected that stretch of interstate.  The interstate re-opened early in the evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An atmospheric river, originating from near Hawaii, rode up and over a broad high pressure ridge situated over the eastern Pacific and brought heavy snow to central Idaho. By the 8th, rising snow levels shut off snowfall at the lower elevations of central Idaho, while areas of Lemhi County continued to snow at all elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated 2 to 4 feet of snow fell in higher elevations, while the Dixie COOP measured a single day record of 30 inches. In addition, as lower elevations warmed, snow changed to rain which caused mudslides on US-12 between mile-markers 93 to 97, which are 17 to 21 miles east of the Kooskia area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were widespread snow accumulations of 10 to 20 inches, with up to 3 feet of snow that fell at the Williams Creek Summit over a 2 day period. Schools were closed for the first time during the 2019-2020 winter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front brought very strong winds to southeast and central Idaho which caused blowing dust and closed roadways.   Power outages occurred and wind gusts ranging from 50 to 75 mph were widespread.   Some trees were downed in the event as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts reached 68 mph in Roberts with widespread 50 to 70 mph gusts.   Interstate 15 closed at 1:58 PM MDT from Idaho Falls to Roberts between exit 118 and exit 143 due to blowing dust and zero visibility.   Four miles south of Lewisville a downed power line was reported with a tree blocking the road near 3431 E and 100 N near the Grant Church.  Power poles were reported arcing in Ririe.  Three miles west of Egin a tree fell on a house with power lines wrapped in the tree.  A semi-trailer was blown over on Idaho Route 33 near Teton.   Idaho Route 33 shut down from Newdale to Tetonia due to zero visibility and blowing dust.  Power lines were downed in Parker at 133 East Main.   A tree was downed at 205 West Main in Saint Anthony.  1200 customers lost their power in the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent area of low pressure along the boundary of a stalled Arctic front set the stage for another round of heavy snow accumulations over north Idaho and northeast Washington during the third week of January.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public near Dover reported 11.0 inches of new snow accumulation from this storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent area of low pressure along the boundary of a stalled Arctic front set the stage for another round of heavy snow accumulations over north Idaho and northeast Washington during the third week of January.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Schweitzer Mountain Ski Resort reported 15 inches of new snow accumulation from this storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The third week of January brought very active winter weather to the Inland Northwest as a series of surface low pressures tracked along a stalled arctic air mass boundary laying near or over eastern Washington and north Idaho. Occasional feeds of Pacific moisture directed into this storm track lead to periodic heavy accumulations of snow across the region through the week. During the whole week of repeated snow storms many northern valley locations received 2 to 3 feet of snow accumulation with some mountain ski resorts reporting 4 to 5 feet of fresh powder.        ||The first surface low pressure system tracked through northern Washington and Idaho on January 10th and 11th. A stationary front followed by a cold front produced a  period of accumulating snow across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP observer at Kellogg recorded 4.6 inches of new snow accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic front briefly dropped down into eastern Washington and north Idaho on January 13th and 14th. Light snow accompanied this frontal passage with generally 1 to 3 inches of snow accumulation around the region. However the front stalled along the Washington/Oregon border and promoted an extended period of light snow which left a swath of heavier accumulations over the Lewiston area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP observer at Lewiston recorded 5.4 inches of new snow accumulation from this storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Beginning during the evening of January 15th and continuing through the 16th a moisture laden warm front pushed north through eastern Washington and north Idaho bringing light snow accumulations to most of north Idaho. The warm front stalled near the Canadian border promoting a longer period of snow over far north Idaho where heavy accumulations resulted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP observer at Priest River recorded 4.4 inches of snow accumulation from this storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front brought the combination of light snow and wind and various road closures to southeast Idaho.  Snowfall amounts were not heavy and in the 1 to 4 inch range.  Roads closed were Idaho route 33 from Newdale to Tetonia, Idaho 47 from Ashton to Warm River, Idaho 32 from Ashton to Tetonia, US 26 from Ririe to Swan Valley, Idaho 81 from Malta to the Cotterell Port of Entry, Idaho 77 from Malta to Albion, and interstate 84 from Burley to the Utah border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A fast moving cold front brought snow and high winds to the Raft River Region.   Interstate 84 was closed from 11 miles east of Heyburn to the Utah border due to the 1 to 4 inch snowfall and wind gusts of 50-60 mph making it impassable.  It re-opened at 2 pm.   Idaho route 81 was closed from Malta to the Cotterell port of entry due to the high winds and Idaho 77 was closed from Malta to Albion due to the winds as well.   Visibility was reduced to zero for much of the morning .

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front brought the combination of light snow and wind and various road closures to southeast Idaho.  Snowfall amounts were not heavy and in the 1 to 4 inch range.  Roads closed were Idaho route 33 from Newdale to Tetonia, Idaho 47 from Ashton to Warm River, Idaho 32 from Ashton to Tetonia, US 26 from Ririe to Swan Valley, Idaho 81 from Malta to the Cotterell Port of Entry, Idaho 77 from Malta to Albion, and interstate 84 from Burley to the Utah border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front brought light snow and high winds to the Teton Valley.   Snow amounts were light in the 1 to 3 inch range but strong winds again brought extensive blowing and drifting snow which caused road and school closures.   Idaho Highway 47 was closed from Ashton to Warm River and Idaho Highway 32 was also closed from Ashton to Tetonia.  Ashton Elementary School and North Fremont Junior and Senior High  Schools in Ashton were also closed.  The town of Driggs was completely isolated for a short period on the 17th with no open roads in or out of the city.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major multi day storm affected all of southeast Idaho with very heavy snow, road closures, and school closings widespread.   Five day snow totals exceeded a foot in many locations with as much as three feet in some mountain locations.  Some high elevation SNOTEL sites exceeded 5 feet.  Some of the road closures by the 11th and 12th were US Route 26 from Swan Valley to Ririe, interstate 15 from Dubois to the Montana state line, US Route 20 from Ashton to the Montana state line, Idaho Route 33 from Newdale to Tetonia, and Idaho route 32 from Ashton to Tetonia.   Many Idaho school districts closed on the 13th and 14 as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow combined with winds to cause extreme travel conditions across the region.  Craters of the Moon National Monument reported 16 inches of snow from the multi day storm and the snow combined with winds to cause Jefferson County School District to close on the 11th and 12th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the morning of January 7th an avalanche occurred at the Silver Mountain Ski Resort near Kellogg, Idaho.  Three skiers died and two others were fully buried but were pulled alive from the snow. The avalanche occurred on a portion of the ski slope that was previously closed due to heavy snow loading on the slope. The resort reported 25 inches of snow fell over the mountain in the previous 48 hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media reported an Avalanche at the Silver Mountain Ski Resort.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On January 6th a moist warm front passed through eastern Washington and north Idaho bringing heavy snow accumulations to much of north Idaho. Heaviest accumulations occurred in the mountains and in the far northern valleys as well as the Palouse region. The lowest valleys received in general 2 to 4 inches of accumulation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public in Genesee reported 7.0 inches of snow accumulation from this storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening of January 1st a debris flow of rocks and mud cut a railroad line about 10 miles east of Bonners Ferry. A BNSF train was derailed with 3 locomotives and 6 rail cars being pushed into the Kootenai River with two engineers trapped inside. The engineers were rescued without injury. Authorities worked through much of the month to remove the engines and contain diesel fuel leaking into the river. One locomotive was scrapped with the other two sent to a rail yard for extensive repairs. ||The COOP observer at nearby Bonners Ferry recorded a high temperature of 44 degrees with 0.46 inches of rain on January 1st in the hours leading to the debris flow. This rain falling on melting snow is suspected of saturating the soil and destabilizing the slope along the rail line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A BNSF train derailed when a rock slide cut the rail line along the Kootenai River east of Bonners Ferry. Three locomotives were pushed off of the tracks along with 6 rail cars, with one locomotive completely submerged in the Kootenai River.

Although snow amounts were not extreme, the combination of wind with the snow caused extremely hazardous travel conditions.   The following schools were closed on both the 13th and 14th:  Aberdeen district 58, Blackfoot district 55, Firth district 59, Shelley district 60, and Snake River district 52.   9 inches of snow fell in Aberdeen through the storm's duration with 5 inches in American Falls, and 4.5 inches in Pocatello.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds and heavy snow were observed the afternoon of the 6th on the Camas Prairie.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds and falling snow made for hazardous travel on the Camas Prairie. A peak wind gust of 59 miles per hour was reported at the Grangeville Airport the afternoon of the 6th which resulted in the total loss of a local drive-in movie theater screen. A semi truck and trailer was also blown off US Highway 95 about 5 miles south of Grangeville. The highest snow total reported was 12 inches in Cottonwood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought snow and gusty winds to the Camas Prairie and other lower elevations. Very heavy snow fell on the higher benches in Clearwater County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 1 to 2 feet were common on the higher benches with 2 to 3 feet at Cardiff Spur and Pierce.  Periods of hazardous travel occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought snow and gusty winds to the Camas Prairie and other lower elevations. Very heavy snow fell on the higher benches in Clearwater County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Although snow amounts were only 2 to 4 inches, strong winds up to 60 miles per hour led to periods of very low visibility.  Travel was very difficult during the critical evening commute hours.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A multiple day winter storm with heavy snow and high winds shut down many roads in the Teton Valley.   12 to 20 inches fell in the Victor area for the duration of the storm.   Strong winds caused extensive blowing and drifting snow on roadways and made them impossible to clear.  Idaho 32 closed from Tetonia to Ashton on the 11th through the 14th.  Idaho 33 closed from Tetonia and Newdale.   US 20 closed from Ashton to the Montana border.  Fremont County School District 215, Madison School district 321, and Teton School District 401 were closed from the 13th through the 15th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On January 6th a moist warm front passed through eastern Washington and north Idaho bringing heavy snow accumulations to much of north Idaho. Heaviest accumulations occurred in the mountains and in the far northern valleys as well as the Palouse region. The lowest valleys received in general 2 to 4 inches of accumulation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weather spotter in Cabinet reported 6.3 inches of new snow accumulation from this storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The third week of January brought very active winter weather to the Inland Northwest as a series of surface low pressures tracked along a stalled arctic air mass boundary laying near or over eastern Washington and north Idaho. Occasional feeds of Pacific moisture directed into this storm track lead to periodic heavy accumulations of snow across the region through the week. During the whole week of repeated snow storms many northern valley locations received 2 to 3 feet of snow accumulation with some mountain ski resorts reporting 4 to 5 feet of fresh powder.        ||The first surface low pressure system tracked through northern Washington and Idaho on January 10th and 11th. A stationary front followed by a cold front produced a  period of accumulating snow across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Lookout Pass Ski Resort reported 19 inches of new snow accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major multi day storm affected all of southeast Idaho with very heavy snow, road closures, and school closings widespread.   Five day snow totals exceeded a foot in many locations with as much as three feet in some mountain locations.  Some high elevation SNOTEL sites exceeded 5 feet.  Some of the road closures by the 11th and 12th were US Route 26 from Swan Valley to Ririe, interstate 15 from Dubois to the Montana state line, US Route 20 from Ashton to the Montana state line, Idaho Route 33 from Newdale to Tetonia, and Idaho route 32 from Ashton to Tetonia.   Many Idaho school districts closed on the 13th and 14 as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A multi day storm brought heavy snow and strong winds which shut down schools and roads in the Upper Snake River Plain.   Storm  total snowfall for the duration were 11 inches at Idaho Falls, 9.5 inches at Sugar City and Rigby, and 5 inches at Saint Anthony.  Interstate 15 shut down between Idaho Falls and Roberts on the 12th through the 14th due to the very strong winds causing extensive blowing and drifting snow .  School closures on the 13th through the 15th were Bonneville Joint District 93, Idaho Falls District 91, Jefferson District 251, Madison District 321, Ririe District 252, Sugar-Salem District 322, West Jefferson District 253.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front plowed into Eastern Oregon and Southwest Idaho producing high winds across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 65 MPH was recorded at Catfish RAWS, 11 miles northwest of Weiser.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front working it's way through Eastern Oregon and Southwest Idaho produced isolated severe high winds across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Catfish RAWS, 11 miles northwest of Weiser, recorded a wind gust of 59 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Interstate 86 closed interstate 86 for an 8 mile stretch from the Seagull Bay exit and the Arbon Valley exit between the Pocatello airport and American Falls.  Numerous traffic accidents caused a pileup.   Visibility was zero in blowing dust in 45 to 55 mph wind gusts which likely caused the accidents.   Several personal vehicles and semi trucks were involved.   Several injuries occurred and many individuals were transported to hospitals.   No deaths occurred.   One semi was carrying a load of fertilizer and this spilled.   Hazmat crews were called in to respond to the chemical spill.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Interstate 86 closed interstate 86 for an 8 mile stretch from the Seagull Bay exit and the Arbon Valley exit between the Pocatello airport and American Falls.  The interstate closed from 1230 PM MDT until 9 PM MDT.  Numerous traffic accidents caused a pileup.   Visibility was zero in blowing dust in 45 to 55 mph wind gusts which likely caused the accidents.   Several personal vehicles and semi trucks were involved.   Several injuries occurred and many individuals were transported to hospitals.   No deaths occurred.   One semi was carrying a load of fertilizer and this spilled.   Hazmat crews were called in to respond to the chemical spill.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure slowly migrating across western United States kept a moist and unstable southerly flow of moisture into eastern Washington and Idaho on May 19th and 20th. This steered disturbances northward through the Idaho Panhandle continuing to bring cool and showery conditions on 20th. A brief funnel cloud was observed and shortly dissipated in northeastern Post Falls.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief funnel cloud was reported by the public in northeast Post Falls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The passage of a strong cold front brought a line of strong winds in the early morning of May 6th. The winds surged north-northeast with the front impacting parts of the southern Idaho Panhandle. This produced a dust storm from the sudden, but short lived burst of strong winds. Sustained winds of 25-30 mph with gusts to 40-50 mph created near zero visibility from blowing dust in the Lewiston Area of the Panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local broadcast media reported blowing dust and strong winds in the Lewiston Hill area, around Lewiston, ID, with estimated visibility reduced to less than 1/2 mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave disturbance tracked across the Idaho Panhandle on May 17th. With a moist and unstable atmosphere, there was heavy rainfall and some thunderstorms. Some locations across the Panhandle received an estimated 0.5 - 0.75 over an hour period. In addition, there were some small hail producing storms. Flash flooding and flooding according in the central Idaho Panhandle. Heavy rainfall led to water and debris flowing over US-95, south of Moscow, Idaho. Minor ponding and flooding were observed in Moscow as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Lewiston Tribune reported that heavy rainfall resulted in mud and water flowing over Highway US-95, south of Moscow. Water and areas of mud were transported from nearby cropland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave disturbance tracked across the Idaho Panhandle on May 17th. With a moist and unstable atmosphere, there was heavy rainfall and some thunderstorms. Some locations across the Panhandle received an estimated 0.5 - 0.75 over an hour period. In addition, there were some small hail producing storms. Flash flooding and flooding according in the central Idaho Panhandle. Heavy rainfall led to water and debris flowing over US-95, south of Moscow, Idaho. Minor ponding and flooding were observed in Moscow as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Idaho Department of Environmental Quality rainfall gauge measured 0.43 in one hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific storm system with it's associated cold front brought a combination of heavy snow and strong winds.   The heavy snow was mainly limited to the Lower Snake River Plain where 6 to 9 inches of snow were widespread along the interstate 86 corridor from American Falls to Pocatello.   In areas where snow was lighter, it was nonetheless accompanied by strong winds with gusts of 40 to 50 mph which caused many road and school closures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow and wind closed US Highway 20 to the US 26 intersection at the Idaho National Lab site.  Craters of the Moon National Monument recorded 6 inches of snow and the West Jefferson School District 253 closed on February 3rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific storm system with it's associated cold front brought a combination of heavy snow and strong winds.   The heavy snow was mainly limited to the Lower Snake River Plain where 6 to 9 inches of snow were widespread along the interstate 86 corridor from American Falls to Pocatello.   In areas where snow was lighter, it was nonetheless accompanied by strong winds with gusts of 40 to 50 mph which caused many road and school closures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bern and St Charles recorded 5 inches of snow and combined with wind gusts up to 40 mph caused the Bear Lake School District 61 to close on February 3rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On May 20th, a cool and showery pattern began in the northern Idaho Panhandle. Two upper low pressure systems dropped down into the region out of the northwest. These brought rain, with some brief heavy rain, from May 20th through May 22nd. Many creeks in northern Idaho, such as Bonner County, were at dangerous levels this May due to heavy rain and significant spring snow melt. On May 20th, numerous rain gauges measured 24 hour total rainfall of 1 to 1.5. There was measurable rain the following day, but lower totals with an average 0.5 to 1.0. However, in addition to considerable spring snow melt at the same time, this rainfall contributed to rising creeks and rivers, some of which were threatening to property and crops.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Mesonet rain gauge in Sandpoint recorded a 24 hour total 0.93 rain on May 20th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific storm system with it's associated cold front brought a combination of heavy snow and strong winds.   The heavy snow was mainly limited to the Lower Snake River Plain where 6 to 9 inches of snow were widespread along the interstate 86 corridor from American Falls to Pocatello.   In areas where snow was lighter, it was nonetheless accompanied by strong winds with gusts of 40 to 50 mph which caused many road and school closures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow and strong winds combined to close Interstate 15 from Dubois north to the Montana border.   Although snow was not heavy, 4 inches fell 6 miles north of Small and 2 inches fell in Dubois winds gusted to 50 mph causing very dangerous travel.   Clark County School District 161 closed on February 3rd due to the wind and snow.

In areas where snow was lighter, it was nonetheless accompanied by strong winds with gusts of 40 to 50 mph which caused many road and school closures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Only 2 to 4 inches of snow fell but wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph caused extensive blowing snow.  Because of travel concerns the Grace School District 148, the North Gem School District 149 and Soda Springs School District 150 were all closed on February 3rd.

In areas where snow was lighter, it was nonetheless accompanied by strong winds with gusts of 40 to 50 mph which caused many road and school closures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Interstate 84 was closed from the Interstate 86 intersection to the Utah border.  Snow was not heavy with Minidoka Dam receiving 3 inches but wind gusts of 30 to 40 mph caused extensive blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave disturbance tracked across the Idaho Panhandle on May 17th. With a moist and unstable atmosphere, there was heavy rainfall and some thunderstorms. Some locations across the Panhandle received an estimated 0.5 - 0.75 over an hour period. In addition, there were some small hail producing storms. Flash flooding and flooding according in the central Idaho Panhandle. Heavy rainfall led to water and debris flowing over US-95, south of Moscow, Idaho. Minor ponding and flooding were observed in Moscow as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall in thunderstorms produced flooding in a field and basement in Moscow.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front produced 1 to 4 inches of snow with 40 to 50 mph wind gusts to cause extremely hazardous travel weather. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another strong Pacific storm system brought hazardous travel weather to eastern Idaho in the first week of February. Heavy snow fell along with an extended period of freezing rain in the Lower Snake River Plain overnight on the 5th into the 6th. Several high elevation sites received over a foot of snow with some school closures on the 6th in the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An avalanche closed US Route 89 from Montepelier to Geneva on the afternoon of the 6th to remove snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On May 20th, a cool and showery pattern began in the northern Idaho Panhandle. Two upper low pressure systems dropped down into the region out of the northwest. These brought rain, with some brief heavy rain, from May 20th through May 22nd. Many creeks in northern Idaho, such as Bonner County, were at dangerous levels this May due to heavy rain and significant spring snow melt. On May 20th, numerous rain gauges measured 24 hour total rainfall of 1 to 1.5. There was measurable rain the following day, but lower totals with an average 0.5 to 1.0. However, in addition to considerable spring snow melt at the same time, this rainfall contributed to rising creeks and rivers, some of which were threatening to property and crops.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: U.S. Forest Service reported a partial road closure on Forest Service Road 280 near Grouse Creek. This was due to Grouse Creek flooding and flood damage caused by spring snow melt and heavy rain. There were no known injuries or damage to buildings.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall at the end of June likely contributed to a rock slide that closed both lanes of US Route 95 south of Riggins, ID.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 28-30 June 1.67 of rain was measured at the Riggins COOP (45.42/ -116.31). Around 10 AM on the 3rd a rockslide was reported near mile marker 188 on US Route 95 in Idaho. This slide closed both lanes of the highway. A bypass was built and opened on the 9th, however instability in the slope continued to hinder repair (which continued through much of the summer).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Howe Peak fire approximately 4 miles southwest of Howe began on July 2nd around 1 pm  MDT and was human caused.   It was along Highway 33 and burned grass, shrubs, and juniper in steep terrain.   No structures were threatened other than right at the start of the fire and no evacuations occurred other than voluntary in Howe initially.   The power in the town of Howe was out.  140 firefighters were used and the fire reached 7,300 acres.   17 power line poles were damaged.   Idaho Highway 33 closed for a short time on the 2nd between Butte City and Howe.  The fire was contained by July 7th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A widespread, winter system brought snow to much of the Idaho Panhandle on December 30th and 31st. The initial snowfall began overnight into the early morning hours of the 30th. This resulted in difficult morning commutes and numerous collisions and spin offs. Significant snow fell throughout the Panhandle with the greatest totals, up to 6 to 12 inches, in the northern half.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public, 5 miles south-southeast of Dover, reported 10.0 inches of new snow accumulation.

This resulted in difficult morning commutes and numerous collisions and spin offs. Significant snow fell throughout the Panhandle with the greatest totals, up to 6 to 12 inches, in the northern half.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public in Hayden reported 7.9 inches of new snow accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In addition to an occluded front's evening arrival, there were many factors that brought light to moderate snowfall to the northern Idaho Panhandle on Christmas night into December 26th. Significant precipitable water and a surface low's colder air provided conditions for snow and rain/snow mixes across the region. The far northern counties recorded 4 to 6 inches of snow, with 1 to 2 inches, if any, in central Panhandle such as the Coeur d'Alene area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter, 6 miles southeast of Elmira, recorded 4.8 inches of new snow accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 1,500 acre fire occurred at the Idaho National Laboratory Site on August 6th   No structures were threatened and it began near mile marker 259 adjacent to the rest area on US Highway 26 and about 1 mile northeast of the Radioactive Waste Management Complex.   Seven fire trucks and 4 bulldozers from both INL and the BLM responded to the fire.   It was contained by the afternoon of August 6th.

Seven fire trucks and 4 bulldozers from both INL and the BLM responded to the fire.   It was contained by the afternoon of August 6th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Muldoon wildfire began August 16th and burned 374 acres of timber about 20 miles southwest of Mackay.    It was fully contained by August 25th and threatened no structures.  Cause was unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Michaud Creek fire  began August 18th only 3 miles south of the Pocatello airport and burned 72 acres.  It burned grass, brush and torching juniper.   Bureau of Indian Affairs, Bureau of Land Management, and Caribou-Targhee were rapid in their response and prevented significant spread.  No structures were threatened.   Several local roads were closed to prevent the fire spread.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Black Pine Fire was a lightning induced fire on August 18th that burned 1,000 acres 6 miles north of the Utah border near interstate 84 mile marker 270.   It burned grass, brush and juniper and spread very rapidly.   It was finally contained on August 21st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm snapped football stadium light poles at the base at Butte County High School in Arco.  Broken tree branches were widespread in Arco and many power outages occurred in rural Butte County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Passing thunderstorms caused fires in Pocatello.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: It is believed lightning struck transformers  creating sparks that caused two fires in Pocatello.   The first occurred in a field outside the School District 25 main office building on Pole Line Road.  A second fire occurred at the 500 block of North Ninth Avenue.  Sparks from a transformer ignited a grass fire that destroyed a car and spread to the exterior of a house destroying the attic.   The home was unoccupied and was declared salvageable.  About 1,000 Pocatello residents also lost power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning caused the Black Sand Fire near the St Anthony Sand Dunes,  It burned 129 acres but firefighters stopped the spread rapidly.   It began around 6 pm on August 28th and was contained on August 29th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Badger Fire began on September 12th at around 5 pm MDT and continued through the rest of September.   It grew to near 90,000 acres and was located 20 miles southwest of Oakley.   High winds and extremely dry fuels caused rapid spread including long range spotting and fire whirls.   There was an evacuation order for all residents on private land in Rock Creek Canyon and Dry Creek on the 18th but was lifted on 8 AM on Sunday, September 20th.  The Sawtooth National Forest issued the Badger Fire Road, Trail and Area closure on September 15th.   Drones were prohibited over the area.   Main fuels were timber (litter and understory), heavy logging slash, sub-alpine fir, lodgepole pine, juniper, mountain brush and grass.  The fire was around 85 percent contained by the end of September.

Main fuels were timber (litter and understory), heavy logging slash, sub-alpine fir, lodgepole pine, juniper, mountain brush and grass. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Trap Creek fire began September 14th and burned over 2,000 acres located 9 miles northwest of Stanley near the Trap Creek Campground in the Sawtooth National recreation Area.  The fire jumped Highway 21 and necessitated a closure of the road between Grandjean and Cape Horn Roads for a period of time.  The fire engulfed lodgepole pines in flames and caused them to fall on the road.   A forest area closure was put into effect and included Valley Creek Road by September 19th.    The fire was 41 percent contained by the end of the month but 100 percent contained along Idaho Highway 21.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Downed trees were reported along Highway 12 from Lowell to the Montana border. Sporadic power and phone outages occurred at Lowell. Wind gusts were generally 40 to 45 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front passed through eastern Idaho shifting southerly winds to northeasterly with sustained speeds over 40 mph and gusts over 50 mph widespread.    Extensive traffic problems and damage occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A powerful cold front brought damaging winds to the Arco-Mud Lake Desert region.   A 77 mph wind gust was measured 6 miles northwest of Atomic City with 73 mph 12 miles east of Howe.   40 to 50 mph sustained winds were recorded for several hours.  Several mesonet sites also recorded 60 to 70 mph gusts during the event.    A front porch roof was ripped off a residence in Monteview at around 5 pm MDT.  A power pole was snapped in half 8 miles northwest of Monteview.   Large tree uprooted 3 miles north of Monteview with numerous tree limbs down along Monteview Highway.   Powerlines were reported downed with power out in Mud Lake.   Most of this damage reported at around the 5 pm MDT hour.   Several power poles and trees downed along Idaho Higway 33 near Terreton.   A large tree was downed on a house in Terreton as well.   A wind profiler was destroyed at one of the Idaho National Laboratory mesonet sites estimated 200 thusand dollars damage.

The UPR site 8 miles north of Coldwater recorded a 73 mph gust with 71 mph at the RAWS at Raft River and 68 mph 6 miles west northwest of Minidoka. Several power outages also reported in Rupert.  A pine tree was blown down near Milner Dam 11 miles west of Burley around 8:10 Pm MDT.  Damage to outbuildings and sheds reported in Declo around 8:15 PM MDT.   A large tree was reported down in Burley at 8:30 PM.   A large pine tree was uprooted in Springdale area 3 miles west of Declo at 8:30 PM MDT.  Numerous trees and tree limbs reported down in Rupert and Minidoka by the public at 9 PM.  United Electirc CO-OP reported numerous power outages in Burley, Paul, Acequia, Rupert and Heyburn due to the wind damage.  Large tree rported down in Heyburn at 9 PM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Hunter 2 Fire began on August 7th, half a mile northeast of Blanchard, due to an unknown cause. The fire burned 684 acres due to high winds, with numerous road closures, evacuations, and destroyed four homes. The wildfire was fully contained on September 12th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Idaho Department of Lands closed State Highway 41 for 2 miles after Hunter Fire 2 grew to 100 acres and jumped the highway. People were evacuated on both sides of the highway in Blanchard. Four homes were destroyed by the wildfire.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The cost of firefighting resources to contain the blaze was calculated at 937,528 dollars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An active cold front moving through the Northern Rockies produced strong to severe winds across parts of South Central Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Jerome ASOS measured a wind gust of 64 MPH in conjunction with cold frontal passage. Trained spotters also reported wind damage around the Buhl area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple large wildfires in Washington, Oregon, and surrounding states produced copious quantities of dense smoke throughout the month of September. The smoke from these fires severely degraded air quality across much of northern Idaho in mid-September. Numerous communities in the Idaho Panhandle suffered periods of very unhealthy to hazardous air quality.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An air quality sensor in Lewiston recorded unhealthy air quality from September 12th through 19th. It spiked to continuous hazardous conditions on September 12th and 13th. The arrival of a cold front on September 19th brought showers and westerly winds which ventilated the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unseasonably cold air spilled into the region resulting in strong northeast winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusting to 45 mph caused trees to fall on power lines, and this in turn sparked several wildfires near Orofino. These fires spread quickly in the low humidity and windy environment. Later named the Sunnyside Complex, an initial damage assessment included 13 homes, 31 outbuildings, and 27 vehicles destroyed. Highway 12 was also closed for a time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unseasonably cold air spilled into the region resulting in strong northeast winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Appropriately 40 mature Ponderosa pine and Douglas fir trees were blown down along 4th of July Creek Road northeast of Carmen. Several campers were trapped by the falling trees. In addition, around 20 cottonwood and aspen trees were either uprooted or had tops snapped out along Carmen Creek.  Isolated tree damage was noted around Salmon. Power and phone outages occurred because of downed trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous cold front dropped into the Inland Northwest bringing strong winds to the northern Idaho Panhandle on September 7th. This strong front produced localized tree damage in the early onset of these winds during the morning hours. Wind gusts reached 45-60 mph producing large power outages, over 100 trees down, and damage to homes and cars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Mesonet station 2 miles west of Athol measured a wind gust of 60 mph. There were an estimated 115,000 people in Kootenai County who lost power due to downed trees and powerlines from the winds.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public reported large trees down on homes and cars in Ponderay from strong winds. The local utility company reported 13,000 without power in the Sandpoint and Ponderay area due to downed trees and power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous cold front dropped into the Inland Northwest bringing strong winds to the northern Idaho Panhandle on September 7th. This strong front produced localized tree damage in the early onset of these winds during the morning hours. Wind gusts reached 45-60 mph producing large power outages, over 100 trees down, and damage to homes and cars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A state employee reported an estimated 100 trees broken or uprooted around Hope. Several vehicles and RVs were damaged on Hope Peninsula. Time estimated by peak winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: All time high month of September temperatures were set in Pocatello, Idaho Falls, Challis, and Burley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The high temperature at the Pocatello Regional Airport on September 5th was 100 degrees.  This was the first 100 degree temperature ever recorded in September and broke the old hottest temperature record for the month of 98 degrees set in 2017.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: All time high month of September temperatures were set in Pocatello, Idaho Falls, Challis, and Burley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The high temperatures reached 97 degrees at Idaho Falls Regional Airport on September 5th.   This tied the previous record for hottest September temperature ever set in 1950.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: All time high month of September temperatures were set in Pocatello, Idaho Falls, Challis, and Burley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The high temperatures of 98 degrees at the Burley Airport on September 5th was the hottest September temperature ever recorded in the city.   It broke the previous record of 97 set in 1998.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: All time high month of September temperatures were set in Pocatello, Idaho Falls, Challis, and Burley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The high temperature of 98 degrees in Challis on September 5th was the hottest September temperature ever recorded in the city breaking the previous hottest temperature of 96 degrees set the day before.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front passed through eastern Idaho shifting southerly winds to northeasterly with sustained speeds over 40 mph and gusts over 50 mph widespread.    Extensive traffic problems and damage occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A SUV pulling a camper/trailer overturned on interstate 15 south of Malad Summit due to high crosswinds.   The two passengers suffered minor injuries.  A 52 mph wind speed was measured 4 miles east southeast of Weston at 7:49 PM MDT.  A metal roof was blown off a hay barn in Clifton at around 745 pm MDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread snowfall was observed across north-central Idaho as a Pacific Storm system moved across eastern Washington. The heaviest snow was observed across the northern Clearwater Mountains and in the vicinity of Orofino and Kamiah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roadways between Orofino and Weippe on US Highway 12 and Idaho State Highway 11 became treacherous due to snowfall rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour at times. Snowfall amounts include: 3.5 inches Orofino, 6 inches on bench above Orofino, 6 inches 2 miles northeast of Kamiah and 8 inches 3 miles southeast of Weippe.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread snowfall was observed across north-central Idaho as a Pacific Storm system moved across eastern Washington. The heaviest snow was observed across the northern Clearwater Mountains and in the vicinity of Orofino and Kamiah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow impacted the northern Clearwater Mountains during the evening hours of December 30 and during the morning hours of December 31. Pierce reported 9 inches of snow. Furthermore, a foot of more of snowfall was observed in higher elevations in areas off and away from Idaho State Highway 11.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread snowfall was observed across north-central Idaho as a Pacific Storm system moved across eastern Washington. The heaviest snow was observed across the northern Clearwater Mountains and in the vicinity of Orofino and Kamiah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Periods of moderate to heavy snowfall impacted the southern Clearwater Mountains. Total snowfall amounts included 7 inches in Lowell and 4 inches at Lolo Pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts over 40 mph combined with a half inch to inch snowfall to cause whiteout conditions on Idaho Highway 32 between Tetonia and Ashton.  The road closed at 1 pm MST on December 22nd.  The road re-opened later that evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts over 40 mph combined with a half inch to inch snowfall to cause whiteout conditions on Idaho Highway 32 between Tetonia and Ashton.  The road closed at 1 pm MST on December 22nd. The road re-opened later that evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On December 20th dense freezing fog and freezing drizzle caused black ice to form on State Highway 75 between Idaho 21 and Squaw Canyon Road south of Clayton.   It covered a 37 mile stretch from mile post 191 to mile post 218.  All lanes were blocked and the road was closed by 6 am on the 20th.

It covered a 37 mile stretch from mile post 191 to mile post 218.  All lanes were blocked and the road was closed by 6 am on the 20th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system brought heavy snow to the Caribou Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated snow amounts from SNOTEL sites were 12 inches at Giveout and 11 inches at Somsen Ranch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front passing through the Pacific Northwest brought mostly dry and windy��conditions across the Idaho Panhandle on June��24th. By mid-day, isolated��showers and thunderstorms moved into the northern��Idaho Panhandle. With afternoon��convection, there were numerous��strong thunderstorm cells forming in Bonner and Boundary counties. These thunderstorms brought gusty winds, brief heavy downpours, and produced isolated hail. There were reports of hail sizing from 0.25 to 1.5 inches in diameter. With the hail, some locations received an additional rainfall up to 1 inch that afternoon. The winds, with gusts up to 55 mph, resulted in trees down, power outages, and rough lake conditions, especially at Lake Pend Oreille and Priest Lake. Other locations within the Idaho Panhandle, only received a trace to 0.03 inch of rain. There were widespread winds with gusts reaching 25-40 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The broadcast media reported that a 21 foot boat with 10 people capsized on Lake Pend Oreille with 1 fatality. There were high waves due to a passing thunderstorm with an estimated 30 mph wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Pacific coast moisture and lift, associated with a passing warm front, brought rain, light winds, and a funnel cloud to the Idaho Panhandle on June 9th. Increasing rain moved through Washington in the early morning and reached the Idaho Panhandle by late morning and afternoon. It was cloudy and rainy most of the day with light winds up to 15 mph. Locations in the central Panhandle, such as Coeur d���Alene and Post Falls, were overcast mid-day with low cloud cover and a light drizzle. The Pappy Boyington Airport in Coeur d���Alene received a total rainfall of 0.02 inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public reported, through a timeline of photos, a funnel cloud formation over the Coeur d���Alene Pappy Boyington Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds caused blowing dust and the closing of Interstate 15 from mile post 119 to 135 north of Idaho Falls at about 3 PM.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds caused blowing dust and the closing of Interstate 15 from mile post 119 to 135 north of Idaho Falls at about 3 PM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Blowing dust closed Interstate 15 from milepost 119 to 135 from Idaho Falls to Roberts at 3:43 PM MDT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blowing dust closed Interstate 15 from milepost 119 to 135 from Idaho Falls to Roberts at 3:43 PM MDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wildfire began by unknown causes about 22 miles south of Clayton on October 5th and burned 485 acres through October 15th.  It was in the Lost River Ranger district and burned Douglas fir and grass.   It had one Type 1 crew and two helicopters.   Some local forest service roads and trails closed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wildfire began by unknown causes about 22 miles south of Clayton on October 5th and burned 485 acres through October 15th.  It was in the Lost River Ranger district and burned Douglas fir and grass.   It had one Type 1 crew and two helicopters.   Some local forest service roads and trails closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Connor Summit Fire which was located 4 miles southeast of Albion began on October 10th and was contained on October 16th.   The fire grew to 5,200 acres but did not threaten the town of Albion.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Connor Summit Fire which was located 4 miles southeast of Albion began on October 10th and was contained on October 16th.   The fire grew to 5,200 acres but did not threaten the town of Albion.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A car became engulfed in flames and eventually caused a wildfire that burned 1,500 acres of grass and sagebrush 13 miles south of Bellevue near Timmerman Hill.  It did shut down Highway 75 south of Timmerman Hill for a very short time on October 9th.  It began around 4 PM MDT on October 9th and was contained by 9 PM and controlled by October 11th at 6 PM.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A car became engulfed in flames and eventually caused a wildfire that burned 1,500 acres of grass and sagebrush 13 miles south of Bellevue near Timmerman Hill.  It did shut down Highway 75 south of Timmerman Hill for a very short time on October 9th.  It began around 4 PM MDT on October 9th and was contained by 9 PM and controlled by October 11th at 6 PM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Interstate 15 again closed from north of Idaho Falls to Roberts from mile marker 119 to 135 on October 21st.  Wind gusts up to 60 mph caused extensive blowing dust and visibilities to drop to zero.   The interstate closed at 11:30 AM MDT and remained closed until the early morning of the 22nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Interstate 15 again closed from north of Idaho Falls to Roberts from mile marker 119 to 135 on October 21st.  Wind gusts up to 60 mph caused extensive blowing dust and visibilities to drop to zero.   The interstate closed at 11:30 AM MDT and remained closed until the early morning of the 22nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the morning of October 23rd, the city of Pocatello experienced record cold morning|for the date. Officially, there was a record low of 16 degrees at the office and airport. In town (and the part of Pocatello), temperatures dropped to between 20-25 degrees. Police responded around 9am to a report of a dead person. The coroner ruled preliminarily that the man likely died from exposure.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On the morning of October 23rd, the city of Pocatello experienced record cold morning|for the date. Officially, there was a record low of 16 degrees at the office and airport. In town (and the part of Pocatello), temperatures dropped to between 20-25 degrees. Police responded around 9am to a report of a dead person. The coroner ruled preliminarily that the man likely died from exposure.  The occurred at Simplot Square at the intersection of W Center and S Arthur.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Interstate 15 closed north of Idaho Falls due to high winds and blowing dust.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Interstate 15 closed north of Idaho Falls due to high winds and blowing dust at about 3:40 PM MDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought heavy, wet snow to north-central Idaho.  This covered roads and caused damage to trees, resulting in a few power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fifteen inches of heavy snow was reported at Lolo Pass during the event. Other snow amounts include 7.5 inches at Selway Lodge and 3.1 inches in Dixie.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought heavy, wet snow to north-central Idaho.  This covered roads and caused damage to trees, resulting in a few power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Early season heavy snow fell in the mountains causing dangerous conditions for hunters and other recreationists. Total snowfall in inches included: 15 Crater Meadows SNOTEL at 5,960 ft., 12 Shanghai Summit at 4,570 ft., and 3.4 Pierce.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An offshore trough swung inland into the Inland Northwest on June 12th. With it, a shortwave took on a strong negative tilt and moved in a moist, very unstable air mass.  This brought strong to locally severe thunderstorms through far eastern Washington and southern Idaho Panhandle. A few large cells moved through the Idaho Panhandle counties of Latah and Nez Perce in the afternoon and early evening hours. These storms produced small hail, winds with gusts to 25 mph, and isolated lightning. Rain was light in Lewiston, but there was a funnel cloud reported outside the Lewiston area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm chaser reported a brief funnel cloud with an area of convection approaching the Lewiston area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southerly downsloping winds ahead of a strong cold front caused damage on the Camas Prairie of north central Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts of 50 to 60 miles per hour were common, with a peak gust of 73 miles per hour, measured just northwest of Grangeville. At least 3 power poles were snapped in half, adding to up to 1000 customers without power. In addition, trees were blown down, debris blown through windows and across roads, and blowing snow occurred early in the event causing 'near-zero' visibility as reported by the Idaho Department of Transportation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong arctic front interacted with a deep trough and brought heavy snow and strong northerly winds with low visibility and drifting snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter west of Grangeville measured 10.9 inches of snow with drifts as deep as 3 feet. There were additional reports in the Camas Prairie area of 10 to 12 inches. Idaho County Light and Power also reported multiple outages due to lines down.

CZ_NAME: LOWER HELLS CANYON / SALMON RIVER REGION
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong arctic front interacted with a deep trough and brought heavy snow and strong northerly winds with low visibility and drifting snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An Idaho Department of Transportation employee reported this event to be moderate to high impact due to staffing numbers this early in the season. At 5:40 pm, an Idaho Department of Transportation sensor at Whitebird Hill reported 'very slick' road conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front plowed into Eastern Oregon and Southwest Idaho producing high winds across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 77 MPH was recorded at Deer Haven RAWS, 10 miles east of Mountain Home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably cold low pressure system brought heavy snow to locations above five-thousand feet across Clearwater and Lemhi counties. Several SNOTEL stations reported their all-time highest, twenty-four hour precipitation totals, over two inches for anytime of the year in Lemhi County, going back to the early 1980s.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Darkhorse Lake SNOTEL reported 19 inches of total snowfall with this event, while 9 inches fell at Schwartz Lake. The Schwartz Lake SNOTEL reported a 24 hour precipitation total of 1.80 inches which is the highest amount reported for any time of the year, going back to 1995. Gilmore Summit saw heavy snow that covered the road with several inches of slush.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably cold low pressure system brought heavy snow to locations above five-thousand feet across Clearwater and Lemhi counties. Several SNOTEL stations reported their all-time highest, twenty-four hour precipitation totals, over two inches for anytime of the year in Lemhi County, going back to the early 1980s.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to two feet of snow fell near Williams Creek Summit, which caused numerous trees to break or fall over the road, making travel impossible for much of the day. Approximately 14 inches of wet snow fell at Lost Trail Pass adversely impacting travel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist Pacific storm brought very heavy snow to high elevations of southeast Idaho with many mountain locations receiving over a foot of snow.   Low elevations of the Wood River Valley also received heavy snow.  Road closures occurred in the Upper Snake Highlands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Icy roads were believed to contribute to a tractor trailer single vehicle crash which led to the death of a 68 year old man.   It occurred on interstate 86 west of American Falls near milepost 33 at 11:12 AM on November 13th.  The vehicle was travelling west bound and slid across the median into the east bound lane and the east bound lane of the interstate was closed for 4 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex early winter storm moved through the region on October 23rd as a deep low tracked southeast over the region. This kept most of the Inland Northwest on the cold side of the storm bringing low snow levels and temperatures at or below freezing. There was heavy wet snow, many with snow up to 6-8, across much of the northern Idaho Panhandle. ||There were numerous collisions and spin outs due to the snow and poor visibility. Trees down and collisions closed many roadways and highways for hours, such as Interstate 90. Tree limbs on power lines created many outages in the northern counties, especially Kootenai County and the Coeur d'Alene area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A COOP observer in Kellogg recorded 7.0 inches of new snow accumulation from this storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex early winter storm moved through the region on October 23rd as a deep low tracked southeast over the region. This kept most of the Inland Northwest on the cold side of the storm bringing low snow levels and temperatures at or below freezing. There was heavy wet snow, many with snow up to 6-8, across much of the northern Idaho Panhandle. ||There were numerous collisions and spin outs due to the snow and poor visibility. Trees down and collisions closed many roadways and highways for hours, such as Interstate 90. Tree limbs on power lines created many outages in the northern counties, especially Kootenai County and the Coeur d'Alene area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public in Potlatch reported 5.5 inches of new snow accumulation from this storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long fetch off the coast of northern California provided the moisture for a significant snow storm and dumped from a foot of snow to locally heavier amounts of nearly 2 feet across West Central Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter near Featherville reported 10 inches of new snow while the public reported 12 inches at Prairie City. Snotel sites reported 12 to 15 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wet and vigorous low pressure system brought snow and strong winds across the northern Idaho Panhandle on the evening of November 12th into November 13th. The heavy, wet snow impacted morning commutes and led numerous road closures throughout the day.  The snow was followed by strong winds across much of the central and southern portions of the Panhandle in the afternoon and early evening hours. Gusty winds were observed in the Lewis-Clark Valley and Camas Prairie reaching 45-60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A CoCoRaHS observer, 9 miles southeast of Elmira, recorded 8.7 inches of new snow accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick hitting system dropped in from the northwest and brought light-to-moderate snow to northern Idaho overnight into Tuesday morning, November 10th. It was not a big snow producer, but numerous locations across the Northern Idaho Panhandle received up to 4-6 in a 12 hour period. Being an overnight event, the snow brought slower, more cautious morning commutes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter in Sandpoint recorded 5.5 inches of new snow accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A new winter system moved into the Idaho Panhandle as a trough dropped into the Pacific Northwest on December 21st. With the accompanying atmospheric river, this system had substantial moisture with it bringing a wet and windy day ahead. With temperatures still too warm for widespread snow, there was light snow in parts of the far north with the remainder falling as rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In addition to wet conditions, the evening of the 21st into the early morning hours of the 22nd were windy. The winds were sustained 20 - 30 mph and gusts up to 35 - 55 mph throughout the entire region. There were scattered power outages due to downed trees on power lines. An amateur radio operator in Athol recorded winds reaching gusts to 53 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A new winter system moved into the Idaho Panhandle as a trough dropped into the Pacific Northwest on December 21st. With the accompanying atmospheric river, this system had substantial moisture with it bringing a wet and windy day ahead. With temperatures still too warm for widespread snow, there was light snow in parts of the far north with the remainder falling as rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Most of the northern Panhandle received 24 hour rainfall totals up to 0.25 - 0.80. The near to freezing temperatures and wet surfaces created isolated black ice and slick surfaces. The Bonners Ferry Herald reported one fatality and one person injured after hitting black ice on Highway 95 outside of Eastport. Their car hydroplaned and overturned into a creek.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall reports along the Highway 95 corridor ranged from 2 to 6 inches.  Some of the higher amounts included 6.6 inches on the western bank of Lake Pend Oreille and 6 inches in Dover.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A widespread, winter system brought snow to much of the Idaho Panhandle on December 30th and 31st. The initial snowfall began overnight into the early morning hours of the 30th. This resulted in difficult morning commutes and numerous collisions and spin offs. Significant snow fell throughout the Panhandle with the greatest totals, up to 6 to 12 inches, in the northern half.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Idaho State Police reported a crash on Interstate 90 near Rose Lake leaving lanes blocked. There are no known injuries or fatalities. Local observation stations and sensors had been reporting snow, ice fog, and mist throughout the day.

The snow was followed by strong winds across much of the central and southern portions of the Panhandle in the afternoon and early evening hours. Gusty winds were observed in the Lewis-Clark Valley and Camas Prairie reaching 45-60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A MESONET station, 5 miles northwest of Winchester, measured a wind gust to 61 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In addition to an occluded front's evening arrival, there were many factors that brought light to moderate snowfall to the northern Idaho Panhandle on Christmas night into December 26th. Significant precipitable water and a surface low's colder air provided conditions for snow and rain/snow mixes across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Most of the central Idaho Panhandle received 1 to 2 inches of snow, if any, especially in locations such as the Coeur d'Alene area. There was a public report of 4.0 inches of snow accumulation in Dalton Gardens.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In addition to an occluded front's evening arrival, there were many factors that brought light to moderate snowfall to the northern Idaho Panhandle on Christmas night into December 26th. Significant precipitable water and a surface low's colder air provided conditions for snow and rain/snow mixes across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The heaviest snowfall in the Idaho Panhandle was observed in the far northern counties. Numerous CoCoRaHS observers and spotters submitted reports of 4 to 6 inches of new snow accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A widespread, winter system brought snow to much of the Idaho Panhandle on December 30th and 31st. The initial snowfall began overnight into the early morning hours of the 30th. This resulted in difficult morning commutes and numerous collisions and spin offs. Significant snow fell throughout the Panhandle with the greatest totals, up to 6 to 12 inches, in the northern half.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Coeur d'Alene area received 5 to 8 inches of new snow accumulation from this passing winter storm. Idaho State Police reported several spun out semi-trucks on US-95 on Mica Grade leaving lanes blocked during the afternoon. There are no known injuries or fatalities. Coeur D'Alene Airport had been reporting snow, ice fog, and mist throughout the day. Numerous power outages were reported by Avista Utilities on December 30th.

This resulted in difficult morning commutes and numerous collisions and spin offs. Significant snow fell throughout the Panhandle with the greatest totals, up to 6 to 12 inches, in the northern half.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Idaho Palouse received between 4 to 6 inches of new snow accumulation. This led to poor road conditions and numerous spin offs and crashes across the entire Idaho Panhandle.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The northern Idaho Panhandle received a significant amount of snowfall during from the late morning hours into the early afternoon on December 30th. Local spotters and observers reported 6 to 12 inches of new snow accumulations across Bonner and Boundary Counties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wet and vigorous low pressure system brought snow and strong winds across the northern Idaho Panhandle on the evening of November 12th into November 13th. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow started in Sandpoint and Bonners Ferry shortly after midnight on the morning of the 13th, ending just before sunset.  The snow did change to rain during the afternoon according to the Sandpoint airport sensor, but remained as snow in most locations.  Snowfall observations generally ranged 4 to 8 inches, with 12 inches reported east of Colburn and southeast of Elmira.  A spotter in Spirit Lake reported a storm total of 8 inches, four of which fell in a span of 5 hours.
